channel.title: Comments for page "Site Rules"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Site Rules"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A membership revoke means someone is no longer a member, but they can re-apply for membership.

Being banned from the site means the user cannot re-apply for membership until the ban elapses, and the site content is blocked from their viewing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How is Membership Revocation different from banning? If they're not a member of the wiki any more, they can't post stuff, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is telling people to upvote your own article considered brigading?

item.title: Re: It says "Sockpuppetery" is against the rules
item.content:encoded:
What does that even mean?

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sock_puppet_account

Does that mean Sockpuppets are not allowed?

When a "sockpuppet" is defined as "an online identity used for purposes of deception", yes, sockpuppets are not allowed and are a permabannable offense on the SCP wiki.

item.title: It says "Sockpuppetery" is against the rules
item.content:encoded: What does that even mean? Does that mean Sockpuppets are not allowed? That's kind of the weirdest rule I ever seen, I wanna know the backstory to this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It wasn't a pm, it was an email notification. The email that I am using right now as an account is an old high school email that had no numbers so I could make a wiki account. Today they gave me the notification that it will be deleted because I'm a high school graduate and that I will no longer need it.

Edit: So my old email said I could transfer everything to my main, in which I did. And my main email, I got a notification from wikidot, they noticed the info transfer and gave me a link to verify my main email. Clicked that too, changed the password just in case.

I think this account will stay. Hopefully. I apologize for any inconvenience, it’s been a weird one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To my knowledge that isn't normal; who sent you this PM and what were its contents?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So I got an email of this account that I'm using, and it said that there was a due date to it and it will be deleted soon. But I remember that I can't make any alt accounts or else it will be deleted. I don't want to break the rules that the scp wiki established but I literally just joined a couple days ago. What should I do?

item.title: Re: need clarification
item.content:encoded:
Not entirely sure what you're asking, but the rules for posting procedure on each subforum — Idea Crit, Foundation Universe, etc. — should be stickied at the top of each.

If you need further clarification on something specific then feel free to ask a member of staff.

item.title: need clarification
item.content:encoded: what are the rules about creating a new forum page, like within the forum groups of course, but just a new page?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's not the issue I have.

The issue I have is that "writing an article that nobody likes" is subtly different from "writing a shitty article" without the latter being a troll one.

http://05command.wikidot.com/forum/t-6488968/disciplinary-stephen-clutter#post-3879203 this bit of this particular disciplinary thread comes to mind - the user in question was in fact protected by invoking this particular rule. The article on question was rubbish indeed but the voting process took care of that, and the prior formulation, thanks to the broad meaning of "shitty", shut off attempts at punishing people from writing articles people dislike (or don't dislike, in one particular case from long past) but that offend certain staff members.

EDIT: Really, all that's needed is to clarify the meaning of the new wording to explicitly include these provisions.

Something along the lines of. "Writing a poorly received, controversial or offensive article, as long as causing offense isn't the sole intent (see Trolling section)."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you think someone's been banned incorrectly feel free to message staff or join #site17 on IRC to inform us. We'd hate to do that so feedback is appreciated!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Writing an article nobody likes is not the same as writing a troll article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice job blurring the rules enough to allow leeway to ban people for writing shitty articles. All that has to be done now is invoke the "it looks like trolling" clause even when it clearly isn't, because hey, it's worded that way and :B internally.

item.title: Re: Total Update
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for catching those, Cyan. The inconsistently-bolded colons got to me once you pointed them out.

I'm not sure what happened to the "no roleplaying" rule. Pretty sure it was a rule at one point, or maybe just in the Guide to Newbies. Either way, I'll put that on the list of potential updates to the Update that I'm gonna post on 05. (Not tonight, though, 05 is already choked with proposal discussions.) Thanks again!

item.title: Re: Total Update
item.content:encoded:
Some of the colons in the first few sections are not bolded even though their proceeding words are, including Rule Zero and the next few. (I suppose that's a minor nitpick, but this is a pretty high-traffic page.)

Your works should be original in style and technique. Contact staff if you're not certain if something is plagiarism.'

Stray apostrophe hanging out on the end, there.

Typos aside, a thanks to y'all for making these reasonable and helpful updates.

Edit: Isn't this version missing the no-roleplaying rules? This and all the most recent revisions are missing the no-roleplaying rules. Was that ever actually transcribed on here? What am I missing?

Edit: So tonight is like The Purge of roleplaying being technically legal? I'll resist- nah, I kid, no one do that <_<

item.title: Total Update
item.content:encoded:
After a couple weeks of discussion by staff, plus another week of voting, the Site Rules have been completely updated. Well, mostly updated. It's different enough that the contents were just wiped and replaced, and you should probably give the new version a read-over.

If you have any questions about the updated rules, post them here.

item.title: Update: Revised Escalation Procedures
item.content:encoded: Following the passage of this vote, the ban escalation process has been modified slightly. Warnings will now come before revocations. All other steps are unchanged - after revocation is a weeklong ban which escalates as normal from there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In this case, the user in question actually had made some unauthorized edits, and staff definitely should be notified of those.

That said, you can always message a staff member (see the Contact Staff page) if you notice behavior you believe is problematic, but are not completely certain of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Epic sorry. I messed up on this one. I am without excuse. It looked like he did, but he did not. I can do nothing more than apologize.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually went into and checked all their edits. They're not guilty of deleting anything on any collaborative pages. Are you confusing them with someone else?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No need, I'll bring it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unless I'm mistaken, only the article's original author or a member of Site Staff should be doing that; else it would be unauthorized editing. If you can assemble a message pointing out instances where such has occured, I expect a member of staff would appreciate you bringing it to their attention and address the issue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, what about deleting entries? ██ has been deleting entries in collaborative pages. Is that allowed? Or is it up to staff to do that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Should we make that the default rule on collaborative pages? Or are there some pages where that wouldn't fit in in-universe?

Given that there are some collaborative pages (at least one of the collective lists for extranormal events/unexplained locations or the like) that have special coding, as well as the example you've given, I feel like "read the instructions on collab pages before posting" should be the default rule (and it effectively already is).

If anyone notices people adding entries to the middle of the list and staff have not addressed the matter, please notify a staff member.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Church313 was a user who added entries to popular collaborative pages for a while. He often added them to the middle of the list. Though, recently staff members moved them to the end of the list. They said, "Please add new entries to the end of the list. Should we make that the default rule on collaborative pages? Or are there some pages where that wouldn't fit in in-universe? Like, for 2305 it might not make sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This. This this this this this.

This isn't something handled by a new rule, it's something handled by you behaving in accordance with the rules we already have.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it would be smart to at least mention it's not a good idea to talk about anything in the "controversial" category.

The issue wasn't that it was a controversial topic. The issue was that it was completely tactless.

You can handle the fine china, so long as you handle it with the care it requires. But if you start mock-throwing it, don't expect people to be ok with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just kinda throwing this out there, but I think there should be a rule (or at least a note if it should be simply unwritten) about "controversial topics".

From the shitstorm I just went through for something of the sorts, it would be smart to at least mention it's not a good idea to talk about anything in the "controversial" category.
It would save heat on those who'd think about it, but are smart enough not to if they read it here. (Definitely would have kept a joke to myself and saved me the hassle if it was).

I've learned my lesson, but it could save others from getting into a similar situation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Do we have a rule (written or unwritten) about not importing drama? In other words, bringing conflicts from other sites onto this one?

What about exporting drama?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Do we have a rule (written or unwritten) about not importing drama? In other words, bringing conflicts from other sites onto this one?

Or would that fall under "conduct unbecoming a site member"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a little unsure about the comma use, but the sentence does seem to read smoother without it. Fixed, and thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, speaking of finding mistakes…

Call for Rewrite: Anyone wanting to volunteer to rewriting a page or discuss the rewriting of a page may respond to this post.

Should be rewrite

and…

or other, severe misconducts.

I am contesting the use of the comma after other

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's been fixed. Thanks for the heads up!

If you find a mistake in a major site page again, don't be sorry to point things out! ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm terribly sorry to point this out, but

"This make take the form of Staff Posts replying to you in the forums; this means that you have the responsibility of returning to recent conversations "

I believe that make should be may.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, Open Staff Posts can be responded to in general. That being said, it is a staff post that's designed to elicit a specific reaction from specific people, so as a rule, it's best as a policy:

a) not to respond to staff posts unless they concern you specifically, and
b) to be 100% serious when working with staff posts. They're not being posted to fuck around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, if you see a post with the title "STAFF POST - OPEN", may you than comment?

If no: What's the different between STAFF POST/STAFF POST-OPEN/STAFF POST-CLOSED
This article is confusing me and I rather get help than regaining a warning due spammy posts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok thank you

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Generally, staff encourages people to use only one thread per draft. Since you're thread is only a few days old, I say comment in the thread.

Remember, you should only make new comments to announce substantial changes. You don't need to announce every time you fix spelling or add a little content.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I made an update to a draft, I posted in the help forum a few days ago, am I allowed to Repost it? Or do I comment in the drafts comment section? I made edits and I want people to review it.

item.title: Re: Not That Important Post
item.content:encoded:
As far as I can see, that's a feature, not a bug.

A large proportion of comments on an article are implicit "vote brigading" because the author either points out virtues or flaws of an article, obviously in the hope others read it and react accordingly. Making a fuss when someone says so explicitly would be kinda silly.

Though, I have seen worse from the site, so who knows.

item.title: Re: Not That Important Post
item.content:encoded:
If I may; I interpret "Advocating for voting on an article for reasons other than content is strictly against the rules" to include brigading. It's noted that you can advocate to down-vote bad articles, but if you advocate down-voting SCP-045 because you want to see something else on the first hundred, I hope they throw your ass out so hard you bounce.

Not you, Rioghail, but you get what I mean.

Definition for those like me that had to look it up:

http://www.thefreedictionary.com/brigading

item.title: Re: Not That Important Post
item.content:encoded:
I'm not seeing how this prohibits vote brigading.

If I say 'this article is shit, y'all should go downvote it', I'm not actually breaking the rules as they currently stand, since I'm advocating downvotes because I think the article isn't very good.
The only thing this actually seems to prohibit is stuff like 'this article was written by Rioghail, y'all should go downvote it because fuck that guy'. If I said 'This article is bad and I hope others also downvote it because it's a bad article and I don't want it on the site' then surely I'm commenting on the content of the article? Would I be in danger of disciplinary action if I were to post something like that in an article's comments?

item.title: Not That Important Post
item.content:encoded:
There has been a small change to the text of "voting" to clarify an issue.

Previously, the text of the "Voting" rule stated:

Voting: You may vote for any reason you like, so long as your reasons are based on the content of the article. Do not downvote/upvote based on your opinion of the author, an effort to manipulate averages, or trying to win contests through attrition. This is considered malicious voting.

The text now reads:

Voting: You may vote for any reason you like, so long as your reasons are based solely on the content of the article. Do not downvote/upvote based on your opinion of the author, an effort to manipulate averages, or trying to win contests through attrition, as this is considered malicious voting. Advocating for voting on an article for reasons other than content is strictly against the rules.

It was always intended for "an effort to manipulate averages" to include "vote brigading", but it hasn't been interpreted as so, and several staff members have made convincing arguments that clarification was needed. Thanks.

item.title: Re: Sock puppetry?
item.content:encoded: Making a new, second account, usually to circumvent a ban, and pretending it's somebody else using it.

item.title: Sock puppetry?
item.content:encoded: What exactly is sock puppetry anyway?

item.title: Another Important Post
item.content:encoded:
There has been a fairly significant change to the idea of "Staff Posts." The new rule on Moderative Posts is as follows:

Moderative Posts: Moderative posts will have a specific title. These titles are used to indicate specific types of posts, and should only be responded to in certain situations. This exact text will be in the post itself:

  • Call for Rewrite: Anyone wanting to volunteer to rewriting a page or discuss the rewriting of a page may respond to this post.
  • Deletion Vote: Anyone wanting to request a stay of deletion or ask for the opportunity to rewrite may respond to this post. Do not 'vote' if you are not staff.
  • Stop Order: Do not respond to this post.

Why, yes. We did realize that there are legitimate times when responding to a post by a member of staff is a good idea.

On a side note, you can and should expect staff to still use "Staff Post" as a title if they're wanting to catch the attention of someone or someones but not implementing disciplinary action.

item.title: Important Post
item.content:encoded: This page has been seriously rewritten, in order to make it much easier to read and digest. Please give it a quick look over, if you have not done so already. Questions about this change can be directed to myself, TroyL, or thedeadlymoose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm writing on an iPad, sorry..
*holds up iPad as a means of self defense*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, now you I can't speak French either

*twitch*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, now you know I can't speak French either

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's French for "white card". It means "free license". The sentence could be re-written as:

That said, DO NOT take this statement as unqualified permission to start squirting out a new article every week just to see what'll survive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "carte blanche"? Sorry, I don't speak Latin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At the risk of interpreting it wrong, I'd say the thing that separates a user who just happened to write a series of bad scips from a user who spams the site with shitty scips is that the former listens to feedback and tries to incorporate said feedback in the next scip he writes, whereas the latter goes "neener, neener, fuck you all." and keeps on writing the exact same crap time and time again.

Edit: Seriously, fuck my typing skills on this phone. Sniped AGAIN.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To paraphrase: "We know shit when we see it."

If someone is posting an article every week and they're all getting deleted in short order, then yes, that could be considered spammy and could lead to being banned. If they're posting an article every week that's floundering but not actually deleted, we'd probably not ban. That said, DO NOT take this statement as carte blanche to start squirting out a new article every week just to see what'll survive. Make sure that anything you put out there is something you actually want to see stick around.

Also, a few mistakes usually aren't enough to get an article deleted, simply because SPaG1 errors are so easy to correct.

Footnotes
1. Spelling, Punctuation and Grammar

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Writing shitty SCPs (although spamming the site with shitty SCPs can be considered an instant permaban.)

What would count as spamming? If, say, you submitted a finished article with a few mistakes every week, would you get banned?
Or just uploading crap that it just two sentences long and makes no sense?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you ever have further questions, feel free to message a staff member.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks so much for the help! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've already replied to your other thread.

…is that possible, or are the documents I post (relatively) safe from plagiarism?

Wikidot tracks edits. If you make a sandbox page, you'll be able to see every edit ever made on that page by any user who edited the text, through the "History" option. If you notice that a user other that yourself has been making edits, inform the staff, and we'll take care of it.

Again, if you see that anyone has taken your work and posted it to the site, if you can provide enough proof of the work being stolen, you shouldn't need to worry.

Besides, this is a wiki. No one's getting paid for this. Furthermore, if someone takes an idea written by someone else and is given critique on it, chances are if they're desperate enough to steal work, they might not understand how to fix it, and the entire thing is just a bad scenario for them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As far as I am aware, that is not allowed at all. When you post a draft, that concept as it is presented is yours, and you have full permission to do whatever you want with it. If someone uses it without your consent and claims it is their own, you have the right to talk to staff about it, and action will be taken against that user.

Nobody wants to see you doing all the hard work for someone else to take all the glory. Your drafts should be safe. :)
